100% natural Ayurvedic formula; key botanicals turmeric and boswellia
No glucosamine and no shellfish, unlike most joint products
4 capsules a day; a 30-day refill is $28

personas.name: Active Retiree
personas.lifeContext: Gardening, golf, daily walks, travel with the grandkids. Measures a good day by how much she got to do.
personas.desire: To keep doing everything she loves without her body setting the limit.
personas.innerMonologue: Some mornings I just lie there for a minute before I try to get up. Not because I'm tired — because I'm bracing. Waiting to find out what version of my body showed up today. I don't tell anyone that. I just... wait. Then I swing my legs over and I move through it, because that's what I do. But there was a time I didn't have to think about getting out of bed at all. I just got up. I miss that so much it almost embarrasses me to admit it. I'm not old. I refuse to be old. I planted thirty tulip bulbs last October, I walked eighteen holes with my son-in-law, I carried Mila on my back at the botanical garden. Nobody is taking that from me. Nobody. But my body keeps... negotiating. Offering me a smaller life in tiny increments, like if it does it slowly enough I won't notice. I notice. I notice every single time I hesitate at the bottom of a staircase and then pretend I was just checking my phone. I notice when my daughter watches me stand up from a low chair and I catch that half-second look on her face — the one I never want to see again. I've tried things. That glucosamine everyone swears by? Months of those horse-pill capsules that smelled like the sea. Nothing. Absolutely nothing. Which made me feel stupid for believing the hype, and then scared, because if that didn't work, what does? I just want to feel like water moving again. Fluid. Easy. Like I used to feel walking into a room — like my body was just quietly there, doing its job, getting out of my way so I could live.
personas.socialComparison: She has a friend — Carol, 67 — who moves through a room like she's forty-five. Gets up from restaurant chairs in one smooth motion. Takes the stairs without a thought. She watches Carol do it and feels a hot flash of something she doesn't want to call envy, so she calls it inspiration. But it's envy. She wonders what Carol has that she doesn't. Better genes? Some supplement she hasn't heard of? Or did Carol just get lucky, and luck is all any of this ever was?
unspokenFears: That the shrinking has already started and she just hasn't named it yet — that every activity she quietly stopped doing is a door that closed a little more permanently than she admitted to herself.
That her grandchildren will begin to remember her as the grandmother who sat and watched rather than the one who got down on the ground, hiked the trail, and kept up.
That her husband or close friends have already quietly adjusted their expectations of her — planned trips around what she can manage — and have simply been too kind to say so.

personas.name: Weekend Warrior
personas.lifeContext: Pickleball, tennis, lifting, long hikes. Plays hard on weekends, then moves like a rusty gate for two days after.
personas.desire: To recover fast and keep competing without feeling every game the next morning.
personas.innerMonologue: Sunday night and I'm already dreading Monday morning. Not work — my *knees*. I played three sets and a full round of pickleball today and it was glorious, I felt like myself, I felt *alive*. But I know what's coming. I know I'll swing my legs out of bed tomorrow and there'll be that horrible half-second where my body just… refuses. Like a car that won't turn over. I'll stand at the top of the stairs and grip the railing like my mother used to and I will hate myself a little for it. Nobody sees that part. They see me crushing a backhand at the net. They don't see me sitting on the edge of the bathtub at 6 a.m., waiting for my hips to remember what they're supposed to do. I've tried everything. The glucosamine horse pills that tasted like the ocean and did absolutely nothing except make me burp. The foam rollers, the stretching routines I print out and do for four days and then abandon. I keep telling myself it's just tightness, just dehydration, just that I need to warm up better. But somewhere quiet and honest inside me I know: the body I'm negotiating with now is not the one I had at 38. And I am not ready to accept that. I'm not ready to be the cautious one, the one who says "oh, I don't do hikes longer than five miles anymore." I watched Karen at the club last weekend — she's 58, she played four matches back to back, and she bounded up to the parking lot like she was 30. What is she doing that I'm not? Is it genetics? Is she lying about how she feels? I smiled and said "great game" and then sat in my car and felt something I am not proud of. I want my fluid self back. The one who took the stairs two at a time without thinking about it. The one who woke up Sunday ready to go again, not conducting a silent damage assessment before her feet hit the floor.
personas.socialComparison: She watches women her age — and older — move through the world with an ease that feels almost taunting. Karen at the club, the woman in the trail-running group who's 57 and floats downhill. She tracks them the way you track a stock you wish you'd bought. She tells herself they have better genetics, better luck, a different job, less stress. She never says out loud: *I want what she has*. But she studies them. She files away every supplement they mention, every routine, every offhand remark about what they do on recovery days. She is doing research she refuses to call research because admitting you're searching means admitting something is wrong.
unspokenFears: That the shrinking has already started — that I've already quietly stopped signing up for the harder hikes, the longer tournaments, and I've been telling myself it's a choice when really my body is making the decision for me, and one day I'll look back and realize I surrendered my active life in small, invisible increments.

That the people I play with are starting to notice. Not that they'd say anything — they're kind — but I catch it, the half-second someone adjusts their pace to mine, or the way my doubles partner covered for me in a way she didn't used to need to. Being managed is worse than being beaten.
That this is who I am now — that 'recovery days' are just the new permanent baseline, and the version of me that bounced back by Saturday morning is simply gone and I never got to say goodbye to her.

personas.name: Desk-Bound Professional
personas.lifeContext: Back-to-back meetings, long hours at a desk. Notices knees that click on stairs and a stiff, slow rise out of the chair.
personas.desire: To move through the workday without feeling older than they are.
personas.innerMonologue: I keep telling myself it's just the weather, or the new chair, or that I didn't sleep well. Anything but the truth that my body is quietly changing without my permission. This morning I grabbed the edge of the desk to push myself up and Priya was right there watching. I smiled like I was just stretching. I wasn't stretching. I stood up in a board meeting last week and my knee made that sound — that creak — and I saw Rohit glance over. He's 34. He didn't say anything. He didn't have to. I used to be the one who took the stairs two at a time to set the tone before a presentation. Now I time my route to avoid the staircase when anyone's walking with me. I take the lift and say I'm checking my phone. I'm not checking my phone. I think about the version of me from five years ago constantly — she moved without thinking, without planning, without bracing. Now every stand-up is a small negotiation with my own body. I haven't told anyone, not even my husband, how much I've started quietly shrinking my life. The weekend hiking group I keep "meaning to rejoin." The spontaneous post-dinner walks I've been declining. I say I'm tired. I am tired — but that's not all of it. The capsules I tried before did absolutely nothing. Forty-two dollars and a lot of fish-smelling burps. I felt stupid for hoping. I don't want to feel stupid again. But I also cannot keep bracing myself every time I stand up from a chair like I'm defusing a bomb. I want to just... move. Like myself. Like the person I still am inside this body that seems to have gotten the wrong memo.
personas.socialComparison: She watches a 38-year-old colleague bound up two flights of stairs mid-conversation, laptop under one arm, not even slightly winded or hesitant, and feels a hot flash of something she won't name as envy. She names it 'admiration.' It isn't just admiration. She also catches herself studying other women her age in the office — how do they stand up from low chairs? Do they brace? Do they pause? She is quietly auditing every woman within five years of her age for signs that this is universal and she is not alone. She needs to not be alone in this. She also has a friend who just completed a half-marathon at 46 and posted about it, and she liked the photo immediately and then put her phone face-down.
unspokenFears: That the stiffness and the clicking are only going one direction — that this is the beginning of a long, slow loss of mobility, not a phase she can reverse.
That her colleagues — especially the younger ones — are quietly recalibrating their perception of her competence and vitality based on these small physical tells she can't hide.
That she has already become 'the older one in the room' without anyone announcing it, and that her body is the thing that gave it away first.

personas.name: Hands-On Grandparent
personas.lifeContext: Babysits grandkids, gets down on the floor to play, hauls car seats and strollers.
personas.desire: To get down on the floor with the grandkids — and get back up just as easily.
personas.innerMonologue: I used to be the fun one. The one who got all the way down on the floor, built the block towers, crawled through the blanket forts. Now I'm calculating every single movement before I make it — is that low enough? Can I get back up without grabbing the coffee table? Without making that face? Lila is three years old and she does not need to see her Nani wince. She just needs me there, on the floor, present, laughing. And I am so scared that "there" is starting to mean the couch. I tell myself it's fine, I'm just tired. But I know it isn't tiredness. My body used to be this invisible, cooperative thing that just did what I asked. Now it's this loud, reluctant stranger I have to negotiate with every single morning before I've even had my tea. I spent months on those glucosamine pills — the big horse-pill ones that smelled faintly of the sea — and felt absolutely nothing except foolish for hoping. I don't want to be someone who takes eleven supplements. I don't want to be someone who "has a condition." I don't want to call it anything. I just want my joints to remember what they used to know.
personas.socialComparison: Her sister-in-law, two years older, was down on the grass at the grandkids' birthday party last month — cross-legged, easy, laughing — and popped right back up like it was nothing. She didn't even use her hands. She watched her do it and felt a hot, private flash of something she'd never say out loud: envy, sharp and specific. She smiled and clapped and handed out cake, and said nothing. Later she googled "why is it hard to get up from the floor at 55" at 11pm and closed the tab before finishing the results.
unspokenFears: That the grandkids will start to notice — that Nani doesn't get on the floor anymore, that Nani always picks the chair, that Nani holds the railing — and that will become just… who I am to them. Their memory of me will be someone who watched instead of played.

That this is the beginning of a long, one-way door. That every small thing I quietly stop doing is a door I'm closing and never opening again. The fort-building. The piggyback ride. The spontaneous chase around the yard. Gone one by one, so gradually no one throws a funeral for any of it.

That other women my age — the ones in yoga pants at school pickup, the ones posting hiking photos — are simply built differently, or luckier, and that I already missed some window I didn't know was closing.

brandVoice.notes: No disease claims. No before/after implying medical treatment. Never show a person in active distress or pain. Talk about freedom of movement and everyday moments, not symptoms.

constraints: People shown should be 40–60+ adults in aspirational poses; from behind is preferred (no face needed).
Show the product bottle or capsules naturally in scene; keep the trust stack visible where possible.
Text-heavy static images do not perform — keep on-image text minimal.
Brand colors: navy blue (#0A1628) with gold/white accents; default warm color grading.
Always qualify the Collagen Type II claim as 'pre-clinical'.
